topic: BOU 2017 eclipse -10.9 nT — verdict flip to Self-Contradicted, text not updated
change_summary: Verdict changed from Std Model Explains to Self-Contradicted (EXP-078 refraction cascade, approved by human 2026-04-12). Evidence text expanded 129% (1089→3677 chars, includes duplicated paragraph). Verdict text expanded 353% (171→1236 chars). TLDRs not updated. Detail_group member WIN-025 retains old verdict.
our_argument_summary: WIN-010 claims the dome predicted the -10.9 nT eclipse magnetic anomaly at Boulder (BOU) in 2017. Our original verdict was 'Std Model Explains' (Chapman ionospheric mechanism, known since 1933, quantitatively predicts the signal). On 2026-04-12, the verdict was flipped to 'Self-Contradicted' via EXP-078 (refraction cascade: eclipse geometry requires definite physical sun altitude, which varies 83% seasonally due to n(r), making the dome's eclipse predictions indeterminate). However, the detail_evidence, detail_verdict_text, and TLDRs were NOT updated to explain the self-contradiction. The text still argues 'Non-discriminating' (Std Model Explains). A reader sees 'Self-Contradicted' verdict with evidence that argues something entirely different.

relevant_files: raw-text/03-predictions.txt (eclipse formula, FSF values, station predictions)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: WIN-010 is a STATIC CONFIRMED OBSERVATION (BOU 2017 -10.9 nT). It becomes the baseline input for future eclipse predictions via formula: delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF. FSF values are hardcoded per-station constants (range 0.642–2.075). Coverage percentages come from standard Besselian eclipse geometry. No live monitoring occurs for this WIN.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: The -10.9 nT value is a hardcoded observation record. FSF values are hardcoded station-by-station constants (1.029, 1.001, 1.722, 2.075, 1.471, 1.529, 0.642, 1.325, 1.008). The claim that FSF derives from 'V12 H(r)/r dome geometry' is stated without derivation. No monitor.py domain handles this WIN.
data_sources_used: Standard Besselian eclipse geometry for coverage percentages
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: 2026 eclipse prediction includes Kp < 2 precondition. 2017 observation occurred at Kp 3–4 (disturbed). Asymmetric evidentiary standard: accepts disturbed-day baseline as WIN, requires quiet-day for future tests.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None — the code analysis from c2 remains accurate. No monitor.py domain exists for WIN-010.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: WIN-010 is a static observation baseline. The -10.9 nT value from the 2017 BOU eclipse is hardcoded into the prediction formula. No dynamic monitoring of INTERMAGNET BOU data occurs.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: The dome calls this 'aetheric perturbation.' Standard physics calls it eclipse-induced ionospheric UV suppression reducing Sq currents (Chapman 1933). Same phenomenology, renamed.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: BOU 2017 observation occurred 2017-08-21. Dome model created in 2024. The -10.9 nT value was observed first, then claimed retroactively.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No derivation connects dome geometry to -10.9 nT. The eclipse formula uses the 2017 observation as baseline input. FSF values are hardcoded without derivation from H(r).

holes_found.description: VERDICT-TEXT MISMATCH: The verdict field says 'Self-Contradicted' (flipped via EXP-078 on 2026-04-12) but detail_verdict_text still begins 'Non-discriminating: the Chapman ionospheric mechanism...' — arguing the OLD 'Std Model Explains' case. The refraction cascade self-contradiction (physical sun altitude varies 83% seasonally due to n(r), making eclipse shadow position indeterminate) is not mentioned ANYWHERE in the WIN-010 text. A dome defender can legitimately say: 'Your verdict says Self-Contradicted but your own evidence text explains a completely different argument. You don't even describe the alleged self-contradiction.' This is the exact kind of sloppy error we criticize the dome model for.
holes_found.recommendation: URGENT: Update detail_verdict_text to explain the self-contradiction. The text should retain the Chapman 'Std Model Explains' argument as secondary but lead with the EXP-078 refraction cascade: 'Self-Contradicted: The dome's eclipse geometry requires a definite physical sun altitude, but the dome's own n(r) formula makes this altitude seasonally indeterminate (1,975–3,618 km, an 83% swing). Eclipse shadow position, which determines the predicted magnetic perturbation geometry, inherits this indeterminacy. The dome cannot simultaneously use n(r) to explain apparent position observations (analemma, crepuscular rays) and derive definite eclipse shadow geometry from a physical sun altitude that n(r) makes undefined. Independent of this self-contradiction, the Chapman ionospheric mechanism (1933) predicts the observed signal...'

holes_found.description: DUPLICATED PARAGRAPH in detail_evidence. The text block starting 'Note: the dome model's own eclipse validation in monitor.py imposes a Kp < 2 quiet-day filter...' through '...not first-principles derivation from dome parameters' appears TWICE verbatim (positions 751–2033 and 2033–3314, ~1,281 characters each). This is a copy-paste error that makes the review look unedited.
holes_found.recommendation: Remove the second occurrence of the duplicated paragraph. The evidence should flow: (1) Chapman mechanism, (2) no dome equation, (3) Kp filter escape clause [once], (4) FSF spread analysis [once], (5) refraction cascade cross-reference.

holes_found.description: TLDR INCONSISTENCY: Both tldr_evidence ('Eclipses cause small magnetic dips because the moon's shadow reduces ionospheric conductivity — this has been known since Chapman described it in 1933') and tldr_verdict ('The Chapman ionospheric mechanism, known for 90+ years, predicts this signal. The dome adds no new physics') argue the 'Std Model Explains' case. Neither mentions the self-contradiction. TLDRs are what most readers see first. A reader who sees 'Self-Contradicted' verdict but a TLDR saying 'known for 90+ years' gets a contradictory signal.
holes_found.recommendation: Update tldr_verdict to: 'Self-Contradicted: the dome's own refraction formula (n(r)) makes the sun's physical altitude seasonally indeterminate (1,975–3,618 km), so eclipse shadow geometry — which this WIN depends on — cannot be computed from dome parameters. The Chapman mechanism (known 90+ years) also independently explains the signal.'

holes_found.description: CROSS-GROUP INCONSISTENCY: WIN-010 is in detail_group 'WIN-010/025/068'. WIN-010 and WIN-068 are both now 'Self-Contradicted' via refraction cascade, but WIN-025 (2024 eclipse, same phenomenon, same detail_group) retains verdict 'Std Model Explains'. If the refraction cascade makes eclipse shadow position indeterminate, this applies equally to the 2024 eclipse. WIN-025 should either also be flipped to Self-Contradicted or there should be an explicit explanation of why it's exempt.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) flip WIN-025 to Self-Contradicted with the same refraction cascade rationale, or (b) add a note to WIN-010/068 explaining why WIN-025 is exempt. The EXP-078 table only listed WIN-068 (not WIN-010 or WIN-025), so the scope of the cascade needs clarification.

holes_found.description: The self-contradiction argument itself needs scrutiny. EXP-078 argues that eclipse shadow position requires 'definite physical sun altitude' and n(r) makes this indeterminate. But a dome defender could argue: 'The eclipse shadow is an APPARENT phenomenon — we observe where the shadow falls, not where the sun physically is. If n(r) bends sunlight to the apparent altitude, it also bends the eclipse shadow consistently.' This is similar to how GPS navigation works with atmospheric refraction — the apparent positions are consistent even if the physical geometry is different. The cascade argument assumes eclipse geometry uses physical position, but if the dome treats everything in apparent coordinates, the indeterminacy might not propagate.
holes_found.recommendation: The detail_evidence should preempt this defense: 'The eclipse shadow is cast by the physical moon blocking physical sunlight. Shadow position depends on the physical geometry (sun, moon, ground), not the apparent geometry. Refraction shifts where an observer sees the sun, but it does not change where the physical shadow falls. Therefore the physical sun altitude (not the apparent altitude) determines eclipse shadow geometry, and the 83% indeterminacy applies.'

holes_found.description: The finding field still says 'Non-discriminating: Chapman ionospheric mechanism predicts exact signal; dome has no eclipse-magnetic coupling equation' — this is the old summary-table text consistent with Std Model Explains, not Self-Contradicted.
holes_found.recommendation: Update finding to lead with the self-contradiction: 'Self-Contradicted: dome's own n(r) makes eclipse geometry indeterminate; also non-discriminating (Chapman mechanism predicts exact signal).'

stronger_arguments.description: The strongest version of the Self-Contradicted argument for WIN-010 would chain three things: (1) Eclipse shadow geometry requires physical sun altitude → n(r) makes this indeterminate (83% swing). (2) The FSF values (0.642–2.075) require precise eclipse shadow position at each station → the indeterminate physical sun altitude makes FSF values doubly indeterminate. (3) The coupling constant κ = 10.9/6.5 (WIN-012) depends on the eclipse geometry being well-defined → if the geometry is indeterminate, κ inherits the indeterminacy, and ALL downstream predictions using κ are compromised. This creates a three-level cascade: n(r) → physical altitude → eclipse geometry → FSF → κ → all predictions.

The post-hoc argument can be strengthened. WIN-010's verdict_text now mentions the post-hoc test, but the key fact is even sharper: the dome uses -10.9 nT as BOTH (a) a confirmed WIN and (b) the baseline input for its 2026 eclipse predictions. This is the same number being counted twice — once as evidence the model works, and once as a parameter in the model. In science, you can't use your training data as your test data.

kernel_of_truth.description: Eclipse-induced geomagnetic variations are genuine, reproducible, well-documented phenomena. The BOU 2017 observation (-10.9 nT at 17:20 UTC) is real and confirmed across multiple INTERMAGNET stations. The dome model correctly identifies that the signal tracks eclipse shadow geometry rather than local solar time, and correctly notes that SG gravimeters recorded 0.0 μGal (electromagnetic, not gravitational). These are scientifically accurate observations.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The self-contradiction is structural: the dome's own n(r) formula makes eclipse geometry indeterminate. Independent of that, Chapman (1933) identified the mechanism 93 years before the dome model existed, and modern implementations (Meza et al. 2021) quantitatively predict the magnitude. The dome provides no derivation from dome parameters — it observes a known phenomenon and claims credit.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A dome defender's strongest rebuttal: 'Your Self-Contradicted verdict rests on the refraction cascade from Section 1.8, which argues that n(r) makes the physical sun altitude indeterminate. But the dome's eclipse predictions don't require knowing the sun's physical altitude — they use the empirically observed eclipse coverage percentage at each station (standard Besselian elements from JPL), multiplied by an observed baseline (-10.9 nT) and a station-specific FSF. None of these inputs depend on the sun's physical altitude in dome coordinates. The coverage % comes from standard eclipse geometry (which works regardless of your cosmological model). The baseline comes from direct measurement. Even the FSF, despite being labeled H(r)/r, is empirically calibrated to observed eclipse data, not computed from dome geometry. Your refraction cascade argument attacks a derivation path the dome doesn't actually use for this prediction. The dome's eclipse formula is: delta_Z = observed_baseline × standard_coverage × empirical_FSF. No physical sun altitude appears in this calculation.'
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: This defense has teeth because it correctly identifies that the dome's eclipse formula doesn't explicitly compute from physical sun altitude — it uses empirical inputs. The preemption should be: 'The dome claims FSF derives from V12 H(r)/r dome geometry (raw-text/03-predictions.txt). If FSF is truly empirical and not derived from dome geometry, then the dome has no physical model for eclipse magnetic effects — it's just curve-fitting to observations, which is exactly our Non-discriminating argument. If FSF IS derived from H(r), then the refraction cascade applies. The dome cannot have it both ways: either FSF is empirical (no predictive power) or FSF is derived from dome geometry (self-contradicted by n(r) cascade). This fork collapses the defense.'

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: WIN-010 and WIN-068 are both 'Self-Contradicted' via refraction cascade, but WIN-025 (same detail_group WIN-010/025/068, same eclipse phenomenon) retains 'Std Model Explains.' If the refraction cascade invalidates eclipse geometry for WIN-010 (2017 BOU eclipse) and WIN-068 (eclipse ensemble), it should equally invalidate WIN-025 (2024 eclipse), which depends on identical physics. Additionally, WIN-012 (coupling constant κ = 10.9/6.5) derives directly from WIN-010's value — if WIN-010's eclipse geometry is self-contradicted, WIN-012's κ inherits the same indeterminacy, but WIN-012 is currently 'Self-Contradicted' for a different reason (κ denominator vanishing from WIN-013/014 conflict). The two self-contradiction paths should be noted as independent, reinforcing arguments.

quantitative_verification.computations: Verified using Python: FSF values from raw-text/03-predictions.txt (max/min = 2.075/0.642 = 3.23). κ = 10.9/6.5 = 1.677 (rounds to 1.67). H(r) = 8537×exp(−r/8619) evaluated at tropical radii gives 3,617 km and 1,976 km; (3618−1975)/1975 = 83.2%. n(r) = 1+0.20×(8537/H(r)−1) at r=6700 gives 1.235.

deeper_analysis:
WIN-010 underwent a verdict flip from 'Std Model Explains' to 'Self-Contradicted' on 2026-04-12, approved by human, based on EXP-078 (refraction cascade). The flip is scientifically justified — the refraction cascade argument is sound and the numerical underpinning (83% physical altitude swing) is verified. However, the execution is badly incomplete.

The core problem is that the verdict was changed without updating the supporting text. The detail_verdict_text still opens with 'Non-discriminating: the Chapman ionospheric mechanism...' — which is the argument for 'Std Model Explains,' not 'Self-Contradicted.' The TLDRs similarly argue the old verdict. A reader seeing 'Self-Contradicted' as the verdict tag but 'Non-discriminating' as the explanation will be confused at best, and a dome defender can legitimately attack this as editorial sloppiness.

Additionally, the detail_evidence contains a verbatim duplicated paragraph (~1,281 chars about Kp filter and FSF spread appearing twice), clearly a copy-paste error from the text expansion.

The advocate defense (rating 3/5) raises a legitimate point: the dome's eclipse formula (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF) doesn't explicitly compute from physical sun altitude. If the dome treats FSF as empirical rather than derived from H(r), the refraction cascade doesn't attack the formula — but then the dome loses its claim that eclipse predictions derive from dome geometry, collapsing to curve-fitting with no physical model. Our text should present this fork explicitly.

The cross-group inconsistency (WIN-025 retaining 'Std Model Explains' while WIN-010 and WIN-068 are 'Self-Contradicted') is a significant gap. All three are in the same detail_group and share the same physics. The decider should either extend the flip to WIN-025 or document why it's exempt.

citations_verified: Chapman (1933, Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr.) — correct, original eclipse-ionosphere mechanism
Meza et al. (2021, JGR Space Physics) — referenced in detail_verdict_text, quantitative Ashour-Chapman model
INTERMAGNET BOU data — public repository, verified
EXP-078 numerical values (83%, 1975–3618 km, H(r) formula) — independently verified via computation
citations_unchecked: Kim & Chang 2018 — cited in WIN-068 detail_verdict_text but not in WIN-010; noted for cross-reference consistency
